observations.summary: Prima firmly orders the storyteller to begin.
Secunda gently asks for nonsense in the story.
Tertia repeatedly interrupts the storytelling.
Alice sits beside her sister on a bank.
Alice sees little value in books without pictures or conversations.
Alice's sister is reading a book beside her on the bank.
A pink-eyed White Rabbit runs close to Alice.
The White Rabbit speaks about being late, consults a watch kept in its waistcoat, and enters a rabbit hole.
Driven by curiosity, Alice follows the White Rabbit down its hole without planning how to return.
While falling, Alice avoids dropping an empty jar because it might hurt someone below.
Dinah is a cat whom Alice misses during her fall.
The White Rabbit continues hurrying underground and disappears around a corner.
Alice wants to pass through a tiny door into a beautiful garden.
After drinking from a newly appeared bottle, Alice shrinks to ten inches tall.
Alice finishes a small cake in hopes that a size change will help her reach the garden.
The cake makes Alice grow to more than nine feet tall.
Unable to enter the garden, Alice cries enough tears to form a large pool.
The returning White Rabbit is finely dressed and carries gloves and a fan.
The White Rabbit hurries because it is late and expects the Duchess to be angry.
When Alice addresses it, the White Rabbit flees and drops its gloves and fan.
The Duchess is awaiting the late White Rabbit and is expected by it to react angrily.
Alice remembers that Ada has long ringlets.
Alice regards Mabel as knowing very little.
Alice worries that her faulty recitation and confused knowledge mean she has become Mabel.
The White Rabbit's fan rapidly shrinks Alice to about two feet tall before she drops it.
Alice falls into the pool formed by her own tears and begins swimming.
A Mouse is swimming in the pool of tears.
The Mouse can speak and reacts passionately against cats.
Dinah is an effective mouse-catcher.
The Mouse says its family has always hated cats.
Alice persuades the offended Mouse to return by promising to avoid talk of cats and dogs.
The Mouse agrees to explain on shore why it hates cats and dogs.
The Duck is among the wet creatures that swim to shore with Alice.
The Dodo is among the wet creatures that swim to shore with Alice.
The Lory is among the wet creatures that swim to shore with Alice.
The Eaglet is among the wet creatures that swim to shore with Alice.
Alice leads the Mouse and the other wet creatures to shore.
During an argument with Alice, the Lory claims superior knowledge because it is older but refuses to state its age.
The other creatures treat the Mouse as an authority and obey its call to listen.
The Mouse tries to dry the group by reciting a dry historical account.
The Duck interrupts the Mouse's recitation to challenge an ambiguous use of a word.
The Eaglet mocks the Dodo's use of long, obscure words.
The Dodo proposes a Caucus-race to dry the group.
The Dodo organizes a loosely structured circular race that leaves everyone dry.
After deliberating, the Dodo declares that everyone won and deserves a prize.
The Dodo appoints Alice to supply the race prizes.
Alice distributes her comfits so that every participant receives one prize.
The Dodo ceremoniously presents Alice's own thimble back to her as her prize.
Alice considers the prize ceremony absurd but suppresses her laughter and behaves solemnly.
The Mouse begins telling Alice its sad history.
In the Mouse's tale, Fury tries to force a mouse into a sham trial in which Fury would judge, serve as jury, and impose death.
The Mouse takes Alice's wordplay as an insult and walks away angrily.
The Mouse refuses calls to return and leaves the group behind.
The Lory regrets that the Mouse would not stay.
According to Alice, Dinah readily hunts and eats small birds.
Alice's enthusiastic description of Dinah's bird-hunting frightens the gathered birds into leaving.
The Lory leaves with the other birds after Alice describes Dinah hunting birds.
The Eaglet leaves with the other birds after Alice describes Dinah hunting birds.
The Duck leaves with the other birds after Alice describes Dinah hunting birds.
The Dodo leaves with the other birds after Alice describes Dinah hunting birds.
Left alone and low-spirited, Alice cries for Dinah, then hears approaching footsteps and hopes the Mouse is returning.
The White Rabbit returns and anxiously searches for its missing gloves and fan.
The White Rabbit fears the Duchess will have it executed for losing the items.
Alice kindly helps search for the White Rabbit's missing gloves and fan.
Mistaking Alice for Mary Ann, the White Rabbit angrily orders her to fetch replacement gloves and a fan.
Frightened by the White Rabbit's command, Alice runs to carry out the errand without correcting it.
Mary Ann is the White Rabbit's actual housemaid.
The White Rabbit owns or occupies the neat little house bearing the plate W. Rabbit.
Alice drinks from an unlabelled bottle hoping to regain a larger size.
Alice grows until her body fills the White Rabbit's room and traps her inside.
Alice swipes through the window and apparently knocks the approaching White Rabbit into glass frames.
Pat is a helper whom the White Rabbit summons after falling outside the house.
Pat reluctantly approaches Alice's enormous arm after the White Rabbit orders him to remove it.
Bill is selected by the White Rabbit's group to enter the house through the chimney.
Alice kicks Bill up and out of the chimney as he descends toward her.
Bill is dazed after Alice's kick launches him through the air.
The White Rabbit proposes burning down the house to dislodge Alice.
Alice eats a cake made from a transformed pebble, shrinks enough to escape, and flees into the wood.
Bill is revealed to be a lizard.
Bill is being supported and given a drink by two guinea pigs after his fall.
Alice plans first to regain her proper size and then to enter the beautiful garden.
A playful puppy appears enormous from Alice's reduced perspective and tries to touch her.
Alice distracts the puppy with a stick while dodging its playful charges to avoid being trampled.
The puppy delightedly chases the stick until it tires and sits panting.
Alice escapes while the tired puppy rests and runs until its barking grows distant.
A large blue Caterpillar sits calmly atop a mushroom smoking a hookah.
The Caterpillar speaks in a languid, sleepy manner.
Alice feels confused by her repeated size changes and her altered memory.
The Caterpillar judges Alice's altered recitation entirely incorrect.
In Alice's recitation, elderly Father William explains several improbable physical feats before threatening to kick his son downstairs.
In Alice's recitation, Father William's son repeatedly questions his father's unlikely abilities.
At three inches tall, Alice asks to become somewhat larger.
The Caterpillar is exactly three inches tall and considers that an excellent height.
Before disappearing, the Caterpillar explains that opposite sides of the mushroom alter height in opposite directions.
Testing the mushroom pieces leaves Alice with an immensely elongated, flexible neck above the treetops.
A large Pigeon attacks Alice's elongated head and neck among the treetops.
The Pigeon guards its eggs constantly against serpents and has gone three weeks without sleep.
The Pigeon remains convinced that Alice is an egg-seeking serpent because of her long neck and willingness to eat eggs.
After ordering Alice away, the Pigeon settles back into its nest.
Alice uses the mushroom to regain her usual height, then reduces herself to nine inches before approaching a small house.
Having restored control over her size, Alice remains intent on reaching the beautiful garden.
A liveried footman with a fishlike face runs from the wood to the small house.
A liveried footman with a froglike face answers the door of the small house.
The Fish-Footman delivers the Queen's croquet invitation for the Duchess.
The Frog-Footman receives and solemnly repeats the message accompanying the Duchess's invitation.
The Queen invites the Duchess to play croquet.
The Fish-Footman leaves after delivering the invitation.
The Frog-Footman answers Alice's questions with unhelpful literal arguments while sitting outside.
The Frog-Footman intends to remain outside the house for days.
Alice abandons the unhelpful conversation with the Footman and enters the house herself.
The Duchess is in a smoke-filled kitchen nursing a baby.
The Cook tends a large cauldron of soup in the Duchess's smoky kitchen.
The Duchess is nursing a baby in the kitchen.
The baby alternates between sneezing and howling in the pepper-filled kitchen.
A large cat sits on the kitchen hearth with an extraordinarily broad grin.
The Duchess speaks harshly and dismissively to Alice.
The Cook throws utensils and dishes at the Duchess and baby.
The Duchess casually calls for Alice to be beheaded during their argument.
The Duchess sings about mistreating her son while violently shaking and tossing him.
The Duchess throws the baby into Alice's care.
The Duchess hurries away to prepare for croquet with the Queen.
The Cook throws a frying pan after the departing Duchess but misses.
Believing the baby is in mortal danger, Alice carries it away from the house.
The baby completely transforms into a pig while Alice carries it.
Alice sets the transformed pig down and lets it trot into the wood.
Now a pig, the former baby trots quietly away into the wood.
The Cheshire Cat appears good-natured but has long claws and many teeth.
Alice asks the Cheshire Cat which direction she should take.
The Cheshire Cat directs Alice toward either the Hatter or the March Hare and calls both mad.
The Cheshire Cat identifies the Hatter as a nearby resident who is mad.
The Cheshire Cat identifies the March Hare as a nearby resident who is mad.
The Cheshire Cat claims madness because its pleased and angry behavior reverses a dog's reactions.
The Cheshire Cat says it will attend the Queen's croquet game and vanishes.
After repeatedly reappearing, the Cheshire Cat vanishes slowly until only its grin remains.
Alice uses the mushroom to grow to about two feet before approaching the March Hare's house.
A sleeping Dormouse sits between the Hatter and March Hare and is used as their cushion.
The Hatter is taking tea outside the March Hare's house.
The March Hare is taking tea with the Hatter and Dormouse outside his house.
The March Hare offers Alice wine despite having none.
The Hatter poses Alice a riddle about a raven and a writing desk.
The Dormouse can speak while apparently asleep.
The Hatter's unusual watch reports the date incorrectly after the March Hare treated it with butter.
The March Hare dips the damaged watch into his tea while defending the butter he used on it.
The Hatter admits that his riddle has no known answer.
The Hatter describes Time as a person who can control clocks for favored acquaintances.
The Hatter says the March Hare went mad shortly before their quarrel with Time.
At her concert, the Queen of Hearts accused the Hatter of ruining Time and demanded his execution.
After quarreling with the Hatter, Time refuses his requests and keeps his clock at six.
Because it is perpetually six o'clock, the Hatter is trapped in constant teatime without time to wash the dishes.
Tired of the discussion, the March Hare demands that Alice or the Dormouse tell a story.
The Dormouse begins a hurried story about three sisters living in a treacle well.
Elsie is one of three sisters in the Dormouse's story who live at the bottom of a treacle well.
Lacie is one of three sisters in the Dormouse's story who live at the bottom of a treacle well.
Tillie is one of three sisters in the Dormouse's story who live at the bottom of a treacle well.
Living on treacle has made Elsie very ill within the Dormouse's story.
Living on treacle has made Lacie very ill within the Dormouse's story.
Living on treacle has made Tillie very ill within the Dormouse's story.
The sleepy Dormouse continues describing the sisters learning to draw things beginning with M.
Unable to tolerate the tea party's rudeness, Alice leaves in disgust and resolves not to return.
The Dormouse falls asleep as Alice leaves and is being pushed into the teapot by the others.
Alice uses the golden key and mushroom to pass through the small garden door at about one foot tall.
Alice finally reaches the beautiful garden among its flowerbeds and fountains.
Five is one of three gardeners painting white roses red near the garden entrance.
Five blames Seven for causing paint to splash onto another gardener.
Seven is one of the three gardeners painting the roses.
The Queen recently threatened to behead Seven for bringing the Cook tulip roots instead of onions.
Two is the third gardener working on the rose tree.
Two explains that the gardeners planted a white rose tree by mistake and are concealing it from the Queen.
Five fears the gardeners will be beheaded if the Queen discovers their planting error.
Seven fears the gardeners will be beheaded if the Queen discovers their planting error.
The Knave of Hearts carries the King's crown in the royal procession.
The King of Hearts enters the garden beside the Queen at the end of the procession.
The Queen orders Alice's beheading after Alice refuses to identify the gardeners.
Alice loudly dismisses the Queen's execution order and silences her.
The timid King urges the Queen to spare Alice because she is a child.
The Knave carefully turns the prostrate gardeners over at the Queen's command.
After discovering the painted roses, the Queen sentences all three gardeners to beheading.
Alice saves the three gardeners by hiding them in a flowerpot.
Two escapes execution by hiding with the other gardeners in Alice's flowerpot.
Five escapes execution by hiding with the other gardeners in Alice's flowerpot.
Seven escapes execution by hiding with the other gardeners in Alice's flowerpot.
The White Rabbit walks anxiously beside Alice as a guest in the royal procession.
The Duchess is under sentence of execution and imprisoned.
The Duchess earned her sentence by boxing the Queen's ears after arriving late.
During the chaotic croquet game, the furious Queen repeatedly orders players beheaded.
Uneasy about the Queen's threats, Alice looks for a way to escape unnoticed.
The Cheshire Cat reappears at the croquet ground as a disembodied head.
Disliking the Cheshire Cat's gaze, the King demands that it be removed.
Without looking at the Cheshire Cat, the Queen orders its beheading.
The Executioner is summoned to behead the Cheshire Cat.
The Executioner refuses to behead the Cheshire Cat because its visible head has no body.
The Cheshire Cat fades away completely while the Executioner fetches the Duchess.
After leaving prison, the Duchess greets Alice with conspicuous affection and a pleasant temper.
The Duchess insists that a moral can be found in everything.
The Queen threatens the Duchess with immediate beheading unless she leaves.
Frightened by the Queen's ultimatum, the Duchess immediately departs.
The Queen has every croquet player except Alice, the King, and herself taken into custody under execution sentences.
Once the Queen leaves, the King quietly pardons all the condemned players.
A Gryphon is found asleep in the sun and ordered to escort Alice to the Mock Turtle.
The Gryphon says the Queen's executions are only her fancy and are never carried out.
The Mock Turtle sits alone on a rocky ledge, tearful and apparently sorrowful.
The Mock Turtle says that he was once a real turtle.
Tortoise was the Mock Turtle's elderly schoolmaster under the sea.
As a pupil, the Mock Turtle could not afford the school's optional subjects and took only the regular course.
The Mock Turtle describes a pun-filled regular curriculum from his undersea school.
The Drawling-master was an old conger eel who visited the undersea school weekly to teach artistic subjects.
The Classics master was an old crab who taught the Gryphon.
The Gryphon confirms that the Classics master taught Laughing and Grief.
The Mock Turtle's school reduced daily lesson time by one hour each day, beginning with ten hours.
The Gryphon ends the discussion of lessons and directs the Mock Turtle to describe the games.
The Mock Turtle enthusiastically explains the Lobster Quadrille with the Gryphon.
The Gryphon energetically helps explain the successive movements of the Lobster Quadrille.
The Mock Turtle dances around Alice with the Gryphon and sings the dance song.
The Gryphon joins the Mock Turtle in demonstrating the dance around Alice.
In the Mock Turtle's song, a whiting urges a snail to join the dance.
In the song, a porpoise follows closely behind the Whiting and treads on its tail.
In the song, the Snail politely refuses to join the distant dance.
The Whiting continues trying to persuade the reluctant Snail by describing another shore.
Alice recounts her adventures since first seeing the White Rabbit to the Mock Turtle and Gryphon.
The Gryphon orders Alice to stand and recite another lesson.
Alice obediently recites another poem, but its words emerge radically altered.
In Alice's altered recitation, the Lobster is boastful on dry sand but timid when sharks approach.
In Alice's recitation, the Owl receives only the dish and later pockets the spoon after sharing a pie.
In Alice's recitation, the Panther takes the edible portions of the shared pie and receives the knife and fork.
The Mock Turtle tearfully sings a song praising turtle soup.
Hearing that the trial is beginning, the Gryphon takes Alice by the hand and hurries her away.
The Knave stands chained before the royal court under armed guard.
The King serves as judge while seated beside the Queen on the throne.
Bill the Lizard serves as one of the twelve jurors.
Annoyed by Bill's squeaking pencil, Alice secretly takes it and leaves him unable to mark his slate.
Acting as herald, the White Rabbit formally reads the accusation against the Knave.
The formal accusation identifies the Queen as the maker and alleged owner of the stolen tarts.
The White Rabbit prevents the King from seeking a verdict before evidence is heard.
The Hatter appears as the first witness while still carrying his unfinished tea.
The March Hare accompanies the Hatter into court arm-in-arm with the Dormouse.
The Dormouse accompanies the Hatter and March Hare into court.
The Hatter keeps hats to sell and owns none himself.
Threatened with immediate execution, the Hatter becomes extremely frightened and confused while testifying.
Alice begins growing larger again but decides to remain in court while space permits.
The March Hare urgently denies the Hatter's attempt to include him in the testimony.
The Dormouse falls fast asleep during the Hatter's testimony.
The Hatter hurriedly leaves court and escapes the Queen's renewed execution order by disappearing from sight.
The Duchess's Cook enters court carrying pepper as the second witness.
The Cook refuses the King's demand that she give evidence.
When cross-examined, the Cook claims that tarts are made mostly from pepper.
The Queen furiously orders the Dormouse seized, punished, beheaded, and expelled from court.
The Dormouse is removed from court amid several minutes of confusion.
The Cook disappears during the confusion over expelling the Dormouse.
The White Rabbit unexpectedly calls Alice as the next witness.
Alice hurriedly rises when called and accidentally overturns the jury box.
Alice apologetically gathers the fallen jurors and returns them to their box.
Alice testifies that she knows nothing about the alleged tart theft.
Alice refuses to leave court and accuses the King of inventing his rule on the spot.
Alice rejects the Queen's claim that the unsigned verses prove the Knave guilty.
Having grown much larger, Alice is no longer afraid to interrupt the King and dismiss the verses as meaningless.
Alice loudly denounces the Queen's demand to impose a sentence before reaching a verdict.
Alice reaches her full size and defiantly identifies the court as merely a pack of cards.
Alice awakens beside her sister and realizes that her Wonderland adventures were a dream.
Alice recounts her remembered adventures to her sister and then runs home for tea.
Bill is left dazed and unable to work after Alice overturns the jury box and briefly replaces him upside down.
The Queen strikes Bill with a thrown inkstand, and he uses the ink running down his face to resume writing.
The White Rabbit respectfully corrects the King's mistaken claim that Alice's ignorance is important.
The White Rabbit prevents another premature verdict by announcing newly discovered evidence.
The White Rabbit discovers that the unaddressed paper is a set of verses rather than a letter.
The White Rabbit confirms that the verses are not written in the Knave's handwriting.
At the King's direction, the White Rabbit reads the newly found verses aloud to the court.
The Knave denies writing the unsigned verses and points out that no evidence proves he did.
The Knave is made entirely of cardboard and says he cannot swim.
The King begins examining Alice about the tart theft.
The King invokes a supposed height rule in an attempt to expel the enlarged Alice from court.
The King becomes pale and intimidated after Alice exposes the implausibility of his rule.
The King treats the lack of a signature on the verses as evidence of dishonest intent by the Knave.
The King strains to interpret the apparently meaningless verses as evidence about the stolen tarts.
After explaining a failed pun, the King once again orders the jury to consider its verdict.
The Queen declares that the unsigned verses prove the Knave's guilt.
The furious Queen throws an inkstand at Bill.
The Queen demands that the court impose a sentence before deciding the verdict.
The Queen orders Alice beheaded after Alice refuses to be silenced, but nobody obeys.
Alice's sister gently brushes leaves from the sleeping Alice and wakes her on the bank.
Alice's sister listens to the Wonderland dream, kisses Alice, and sends her home for tea.
After Alice leaves, her sister remains on the bank and begins imagining the creatures from Alice's dream.
Alice's sister imagines Alice retaining her loving childhood nature as an adult and sharing strange stories with other children.

relationships.label: sibling
relationships.detail: Alice and the book-reading girl beside her are sisters.

relationships.label: pursues
relationships.detail: Curiosity drives Alice to follow the White Rabbit into its hole.

relationships.label: pursues
relationships.detail: Their immediate encounter ends when the White Rabbit flees Alice into the darkness.

relationships.label: expected by
relationships.detail: The White Rabbit believes the Duchess is waiting and will be angry about its lateness.

relationships.label: acquaintance
relationships.detail: Ada is one of the children Alice knows and considers when questioning her identity.

relationships.label: acquaintance
relationships.detail: Mabel is one of the children Alice knows and fears she may have become.

relationships.label: pet owner
relationships.detail: Dinah is Alice's beloved household cat.

relationships.label: uneasy acquaintance
relationships.detail: Alice approaches the Mouse in the pool and tries to converse with it.

relationships.label: uneasy acquaintance
relationships.detail: After repeated offense, Alice reconciles with the Mouse enough for it to promise its history.

relationships.label: uneasy acquaintance
relationships.detail: The Mouse rejects Alice's apology and leaves without finishing its history.

relationships.label: argumentative acquaintance
relationships.detail: Alice talks familiarly with the Lory but disputes its claim to know better.

relationships.label: argumentative acquaintance
relationships.detail: Their current interaction ends when the birds leave after hearing about Dinah.

relationships.label: expected by
relationships.detail: The White Rabbit now fears the waiting Duchess will have it executed over the missing gloves and fan.

relationships.label: mistaken servant
relationships.detail: The White Rabbit mistakes Alice for its housemaid and commands her to fetch gloves and a fan.

relationships.label: employs as housemaid
relationships.detail: Mary Ann is the White Rabbit's actual housemaid.

relationships.label: commands
relationships.detail: The White Rabbit summons Pat for help and orders him to remove Alice's arm from the window.

relationships.label: commands
relationships.detail: The White Rabbit, identified as the master, has Bill sent down the chimney.

relationships.label: repels
relationships.detail: Alice kicks Bill out of the chimney when he tries to enter the room.

relationships.label: mistaken servant
relationships.detail: The mistaken servant encounter ends when Alice shrinks and escapes the White Rabbit's house.

relationships.label: playful encounter
relationships.detail: Alice engages the puppy with a stick while protecting herself from its enormous playful charges.

relationships.label: playful encounter
relationships.detail: Alice leaves the exhausted puppy behind and escapes through the wood.

relationships.label: seeks advice from
relationships.detail: Alice enters a tense conversation with the Caterpillar about her identity and changing size.

relationships.label: parent
relationships.detail: Father William is the questioning young man's father in Alice's recitation.

relationships.label: seeks advice from
relationships.detail: Their encounter ends after the Caterpillar explains the mushroom's effects and crawls out of sight.

relationships.label: mistakes for serpent
relationships.detail: The Pigeon attacks Alice and regards her elongated neck as proof that she threatens its eggs.

relationships.label: mistakes for serpent
relationships.detail: Their confrontation ends when the Pigeon orders Alice away and returns to its nest.

relationships.label: invites to croquet
relationships.detail: The Queen sends the Duchess an invitation to play croquet.

relationships.label: seeks entry advice from
relationships.detail: Alice asks the Frog-Footman how to enter the house but receives only unhelpful answers.

relationships.label: seeks entry advice from
relationships.detail: Alice gives up talking to the Frog-Footman and enters by herself.

relationships.label: parent
relationships.detail: The baby is the Duchess's young son, whom she handles violently.

relationships.label: attacks
relationships.detail: The Cook pelts the Duchess with kitchenware.

relationships.label: attacks
relationships.detail: The Cook pelts the baby with kitchenware along with the Duchess.

relationships.label: attacks
relationships.detail: The Cook's assault ends when her final thrown frying pan misses the departing Duchess.

relationships.label: attacks
relationships.detail: The Cook can no longer strike the baby after the Duchess gives it to Alice.

relationships.label: rescues
relationships.detail: Alice receives the baby and carries it away to protect it from the violent kitchen.

relationships.label: rescues
relationships.detail: Alice's care ends when the transformed pig is released into the wood.

relationships.label: seeks directions from
relationships.detail: Alice respectfully asks the Cheshire Cat where she should go.

relationships.label: seeks directions from
relationships.detail: Their woodland conversation ends when the Cheshire Cat slowly vanishes.

relationships.label: tea companion
relationships.detail: The Hatter and March Hare share the perpetual tea party outside the March Hare's house.

relationships.label: tea companion
relationships.detail: The Hatter shares the tea party with the Dormouse and uses it as a cushion.

relationships.label: tea companion
relationships.detail: The March Hare shares the tea party with the Dormouse and uses it as a cushion.

relationships.label: tea-party encounter
relationships.detail: Alice joins the March Hare's tea table despite his claim that there is no room.

relationships.label: tea-party encounter
relationships.detail: Alice enters an argumentative conversation with the curious and rude Hatter.

relationships.label: tea-party encounter
relationships.detail: Alice converses with the sleepy Dormouse and later listens to its story.

relationships.label: estranged acquaintance
relationships.detail: After a quarrel, Time refuses the Hatter's requests and leaves his clock fixed at six.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The Queen of Hearts orders the Hatter beheaded after his concert performance.

relationships.label: sibling
relationships.detail: Elsie and Lacie are sisters in the Dormouse's story.

relationships.label: sibling
relationships.detail: Elsie and Tillie are sisters in the Dormouse's story.

relationships.label: sibling
relationships.detail: Lacie and Tillie are sisters in the Dormouse's story.

relationships.label: tea-party encounter
relationships.detail: Alice ends the encounter by leaving the tea party in disgust.

relationships.label: tea-party encounter
relationships.detail: Alice ends the encounter after the Hatter's final rude remark.

relationships.label: tea-party encounter
relationships.detail: Alice leaves while the Dormouse falls asleep and its story remains unfinished.

relationships.label: spouse
relationships.detail: The King and Queen of Hearts are the royal couple leading the procession.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The Queen orders Alice beheaded after Alice answers her defiantly.

relationships.label: protects
relationships.detail: The King intervenes by reminding the Queen that Alice is only a child.

relationships.label: sentences to execution
relationships.detail: The Queen orders Two beheaded for the gardeners' rose-tree mistake.

relationships.label: sentences to execution
relationships.detail: The Queen orders Five beheaded for the gardeners' rose-tree mistake.

relationships.label: sentences to execution
relationships.detail: The Queen orders Seven beheaded for the gardeners' rose-tree mistake.

relationships.label: rescues
relationships.detail: Alice hides Two in a flowerpot to prevent his execution.

relationships.label: rescues
relationships.detail: Alice hides Five in a flowerpot to prevent his execution.

relationships.label: rescues
relationships.detail: Alice hides Seven in a flowerpot to prevent his execution.

relationships.label: court acquaintance
relationships.detail: The anxious White Rabbit walks beside Alice and quietly discusses the Duchess.

relationships.label: assaults
relationships.detail: The Duchess boxed the Queen's ears after arriving late.

relationships.label: sentences to execution
relationships.detail: The Queen has the Duchess imprisoned under an execution sentence for striking her.

relationships.label: friend
relationships.detail: Alice welcomes the reappearing Cheshire Cat as a friend and describes the croquet game to it.

relationships.label: seeks removal of
relationships.detail: The King dislikes the Cheshire Cat and demands its removal.

relationships.label: sentences to execution
relationships.detail: The Queen responds to the dispute by ordering the Cheshire Cat beheaded.

relationships.label: ordered to execute
relationships.detail: The Executioner is assigned to behead the Cat but refuses because it has no visible body.

relationships.label: friend
relationships.detail: Their renewed conversation ends when the Cheshire Cat disappears completely.

relationships.label: ordered to execute
relationships.detail: The attempted execution becomes impossible when the Cat vanishes.

relationships.label: friendly reunion
relationships.detail: The newly pleasant Duchess affectionately walks with Alice after leaving prison.

relationships.label: sentences to execution
relationships.detail: The Queen renews her threat and forces the Duchess to leave immediately.

relationships.label: friendly reunion
relationships.detail: Their walk ends when the Queen frightens the Duchess away.

relationships.label: commands
relationships.detail: The Queen orders the Gryphon to guide Alice to the Mock Turtle.

relationships.label: guides
relationships.detail: The Gryphon is assigned to take Alice to hear the Mock Turtle's history.

relationships.label: listens to history
relationships.detail: Alice sits with the Mock Turtle to hear his life and school story.

relationships.label: student of
relationships.detail: The Mock Turtle attended the undersea school taught by Tortoise.

relationships.label: student of
relationships.detail: The Mock Turtle's school received weekly lessons from the Drawling-master.

relationships.label: student of
relationships.detail: The Gryphon studied under the old crab who served as Classics master.

relationships.label: invites to dance
relationships.detail: In the Mock Turtle's song, the Whiting urges the Snail to join the Lobster Quadrille.

relationships.label: pursues
relationships.detail: In the song, the Porpoise follows closely enough to tread on the Whiting's tail.

relationships.label: invites to dance
relationships.detail: After the Snail refuses, the Whiting continues trying to persuade it.

relationships.label: listens to history
relationships.detail: Alice's encounter with the Mock Turtle ends when the Gryphon hurries her toward the trial.

relationships.label: guides
relationships.detail: The Gryphon completes its escort when they arrive at the royal court.

relationships.label: judges
relationships.detail: The King presides over the court trying the chained Knave.

relationships.label: accused of stealing from
relationships.detail: The formal charge alleges that the Knave stole the Queen's tarts.

relationships.label: court official
relationships.detail: The White Rabbit serves the King's court as herald and manages the witness list and accusation.

relationships.label: examines as witness
relationships.detail: The King questions the Hatter as the trial's first witness.

relationships.label: examines as witness
relationships.detail: The King's examination ends when he dismisses the Hatter from court.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The Queen renews her attempt to have the Hatter beheaded, but he escapes before an officer can reach him.

relationships.label: employs as cook
relationships.detail: The second witness is explicitly identified as the Duchess's Cook.

relationships.label: examines as witness
relationships.detail: The King reluctantly cross-examines the Cook as the second witness.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The Queen orders the Dormouse seized, punished, and beheaded after its interruption.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The immediate confrontation ends when the Dormouse is expelled from court.

relationships.label: examines as witness
relationships.detail: The Cook's examination ends when she disappears during the courtroom confusion.

relationships.label: summons as witness
relationships.detail: As court herald, the White Rabbit calls Alice to be the next witness.

relationships.label: summons as witness
relationships.detail: The summons is fulfilled when Alice appears and the King begins questioning her.

relationships.label: examines as witness
relationships.detail: The King questions Alice as a witness in the Knave's trial.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The Queen renews her execution order after Alice openly rejects her authority.

relationships.label: attacks
relationships.detail: The Queen angrily throws an inkstand at Bill and hits him.

relationships.label: examines as witness
relationships.detail: The examination ends when the card court attacks Alice and she awakens from the dream.

relationships.label: threatens execution
relationships.detail: The Queen's threat ends when the court dissolves and Alice awakens.

relationships.label: judges
relationships.detail: The Knave's trial ends unresolved when Alice awakens from the dream.

relationships.label: accused of stealing from
relationships.detail: The accusation remains unresolved when the Wonderland court disappears with Alice's awakening.

relationships.label: shares dream with
relationships.detail: Alice recounts her remembered Wonderland adventures to her sister after awakening.

summaries.summary: Prima is an imperious listener who orders the story to begin.
Secunda is a gentle listener who hopes the story will contain nonsense.
Tertia is a listener prone to frequent interruptions.

Alice follows the White Rabbit underground, changes size repeatedly, and seeks a beautiful garden. After swimming through her tears, she joins a group of creatures, distributes race prizes, and alienates the Mouse and birds through misunderstandings and praise of Dinah. She ends about two feet tall, alone and tearful, hoping approaching footsteps belong to the Mouse.
Alice's sister remains on the bank reading when Alice's pursuit begins.

The pink-eyed White Rabbit wears a waistcoat, carries a watch, and rushes underground because it is late. It later appears with gloves and a fan, worries about the Duchess, and flees when Alice speaks to it.
Dinah is Alice's beloved cat and an effective hunter of mice and small birds; Alice misses her while underground.
The Duchess is known only through the White Rabbit, which fears she will be angry because it has kept her waiting.
Ada is a child Alice knows, distinguished in Alice's mind by her long ringlets.
Mabel is a child Alice knows and regards as poorly informed; Alice briefly fears she has become her.

The speaking Mouse strongly dislikes cats and dogs and holds authority among the wet creatures. It tries to dry them with history, begins its own sad tale, then leaves angrily after Alice misunderstands it and does not return when called.
The Duck joins the wet party, challenges the Mouse's ambiguous historical phrasing, and later leaves after hearing about Dinah's bird-hunting.
The Dodo organizes the Caucus-race, declares every participant a winner, makes Alice distribute prizes, and presents her thimble back as her own prize before later departing with the birds.
The Lory argues that being older makes it wiser than Alice, regrets the Mouse's departure, and later leaves after Alice describes Dinah hunting birds.
The Eaglet joins the wet party, mocks the Dodo's obscure language, and later leaves after hearing about Dinah's bird-hunting.
Fury is the threatening antagonist in the Mouse's tale who proposes a rigged trial ending in the mouse's death.

Mistaken for the White Rabbit's housemaid, Alice becomes trapped at enormous size in its house, repels Bill, and escapes by shrinking. She evades a playful puppy, receives cryptic mushroom advice from the Caterpillar, survives an accusation from a nesting Pigeon, regains her usual height, and then shrinks to nine inches to approach a small house while still seeking the garden.

The White Rabbit returns fearing punishment by the Duchess, mistakes Alice for its housemaid Mary Ann, and sends her to its house. When giant Alice becomes trapped there, it recruits Pat and Bill, threatens to burn the house, and loses Alice when she shrinks and escapes.
The Duchess remains unseen, while the White Rabbit now fears she will have it executed over its missing gloves and fan.
Mary Ann is the White Rabbit's unseen housemaid, for whom it mistakes Alice.
Pat is a reluctant helper ordered by the White Rabbit to remove Alice's huge arm from the house window.
Bill is a lizard sent down the White Rabbit's chimney; Alice kicks him into the air, leaving him dazed and needing support and a drink.
The puppy appears enormous beside tiny Alice and enthusiastically chases her stick until it tires, allowing her to escape.
The blue, hookah-smoking Caterpillar is terse and argumentative with Alice but ultimately explains how the mushroom can make her taller or shorter before crawling away.
Father William is the elderly subject of Alice's altered recitation, performing improbable feats and impatiently answering his son's questions.
Father William's son is the persistent questioner in Alice's altered recitation.
The exhausted nesting Pigeon guards its eggs against serpents, attacks long-necked Alice, and refuses to believe she is a little girl before sending her away and returning to the nest.

Alice enters the Duchess's chaotic kitchen, rescues a baby that transforms into a pig, and obtains directions from the vanishing Cheshire Cat. After an exasperating tea party with the March Hare, Hatter, and Dormouse, she uses the mushroom and golden key to reach the beautiful garden at last.
The Fish-Footman delivers the Queen's croquet invitation for the Duchess and then leaves.
The Frog-Footman receives the Duchess's invitation, then sits outside giving Alice unhelpful answers and intending to remain there for days.
The Queen of Hearts invites the Duchess to play croquet and is described by the Hatter as having demanded his execution during her concert.
The harsh Duchess nurses and violently handles her baby in a chaotic kitchen, calls for Alice's beheading, throws the child to Alice, and leaves to prepare for croquet with the Queen.
The Cook tends heavily peppered soup while attacking the Duchess and baby with kitchenware, then narrowly misses the departing Duchess with a frying pan.
The Duchess's sneezing, howling son is endangered in the kitchen and carried away by Alice, then transforms completely into a pig and trots into the wood.
The grinning Cheshire Cat gives Alice directions toward the Hatter and March Hare, claims that everyone nearby is mad, and repeatedly appears and vanishes, eventually leaving only its grin.
The rude Hatter presides over a perpetual tea party, poses an answerless riddle, and explains that a quarrel with Time has left his clock fixed at six after the Queen threatened his execution.
The allegedly mad March Hare hosts the perpetual tea party, offers nonexistent wine, defends damaging the Hatter's watch with butter, and pressures the Dormouse to tell a story.
The perpetually sleepy Dormouse is used as a cushion at the tea party and tells an unfinished story about three sisters before falling asleep and being pushed toward the teapot.
Time is described as a person who can manipulate clocks but, after quarreling with the Hatter, refuses his requests and keeps his clock fixed at six.
Elsie is one of three sisters in the Dormouse's story, living ill at the bottom of a treacle well and learning to draw.
Lacie is one of three sisters in the Dormouse's story, living ill at the bottom of a treacle well and learning to draw.
Tillie is one of three sisters in the Dormouse's story, living ill at the bottom of a treacle well and learning to draw.
Five is a card-like gardener who helps paint mistaken white roses red, quarrels with Seven, and escapes the Queen's execution order when Alice hides the gardeners.
Seven is a card-like gardener already threatened over a kitchen errand; he helps conceal the mistaken white roses and escapes another execution order through Alice.
Two is a card-like gardener who explains the rose-tree mistake and escapes the Queen's execution order when Alice hides him with the others.
The Knave of Hearts carries the King's crown in the royal procession and carefully obeys the Queen's order to turn over the gardeners.
The timid King of Hearts accompanies the Queen, intervenes for Alice, demands the Cheshire Cat's removal, and quietly pardons the condemned croquet players once the Queen leaves.

Alice reaches the royal croquet ground, defies the Queen, rescues three gardeners, endures the chaotic game, reunites with the Duchess and Cheshire Cat, and is guided to hear the Mock Turtle's history.

The Queen of Hearts rules the card court through furious threats, condemns gardeners and croquet players, orders the Cheshire Cat beheaded, and drives the Duchess away, though the Gryphon says her executions never occur.
The nervous White Rabbit appears among the royal guests and privately tells Alice that the Duchess was sentenced for boxing the Queen's ears.
The Duchess is imprisoned for striking the Queen, returns in an affectionate mood and searches constantly for morals, then flees when the Queen again threatens her head.
The Cheshire Cat rejoins Alice at croquet as a disembodied head, antagonizes the King, and evades a beheading dispute by fading away completely.
The Executioner is summoned for the Cheshire Cat but refuses to behead a head without a body and loses the target when the Cat vanishes.
The Gryphon guides Alice to the Mock Turtle, dismisses the Queen's executions as imaginary, recalls studying under an old crab, and prompts the Mock Turtle to move from lessons to games.
The tearful Mock Turtle says he was once a real turtle and recounts his undersea schooling, including its teachers, pun-filled subjects, unaffordable extras, and daily shortening lessons.
Tortoise was the elderly turtle who taught the Mock Turtle at an undersea school.
The Drawling-master was an old conger eel who visited the undersea school weekly to teach its artistic curriculum.
The Classics master was an old crab who taught the Gryphon subjects called Laughing and Grief.
In the Mock Turtle's song, the Whiting is pursued by a porpoise and persistently invites a reluctant Snail to the dance.
In the Mock Turtle's song, the Snail politely refuses the Whiting's invitation to join the distant dance.
In the Mock Turtle's song, the Porpoise closely pursues the Whiting and treads on its tail.
The Lobster in Alice's altered recitation is confident on dry sand but becomes timid when sharks approach.
In Alice's altered recitation, the Owl receives the dish and spoon while sharing a pie with the Panther.
In Alice's altered recitation, the Panther takes the pie's food and receives the knife and fork.
The Mock Turtle demonstrates the Lobster Quadrille, sings two melancholy songs, hears Alice's adventures, and remains behind when the trial begins.
The Gryphon demonstrates the Lobster Quadrille, listens to Alice's adventures, orders another recitation, and completes its escort by taking Alice to court.
Alice recounts her adventures, produces another altered recitation, attends the royal trial, begins growing again, and is unexpectedly called as the next witness.
The chained and guarded Knave stands trial before the King on an accusation that he stole the Queen's tarts.
The King acts as judge in the Knave's trial, repeatedly threatens the Hatter, and reluctantly questions the uncooperative Cook.
The Queen presides beside the King as the alleged victim of the tart theft and issues furious execution orders against the Hatter and Dormouse.
The White Rabbit serves as court herald, reads the accusation, corrects the King's premature procedure, and calls Alice as the next witness.
Bill the Lizard is a juror who must write ineffectually with his finger after Alice steals his squeaking pencil.
The terrified Hatter testifies as the first witness, reveals that he sells hats without owning one, and escapes a renewed execution order by fleeing court.
The March Hare accompanies the Hatter into court and urgently denies being part of his confused testimony.
The Dormouse accompanies the tea-party witnesses, falls asleep during testimony, and is expelled after an interruption provokes the Queen.
The Duchess's Cook appears as the second witness, refuses to cooperate, claims tarts are mostly pepper, and disappears during the courtroom confusion.

Alice testifies that she knows nothing of the theft, challenges the court's fabricated rules and unjust procedure, grows fully confident and full-sized, then awakens to discover Wonderland was a dream and recounts it to her sister.
Alice's sister wakes her gently, listens to the Wonderland dream, and afterward imagines Alice preserving her loving nature into adulthood and delighting other children with stories.
Bill the Lizard is knocked from the jury box and left dazed, then is struck by the Queen's inkstand and uses the ink on his face to continue writing.
The White Rabbit concludes Alice's summons, corrects the King's errors, introduces the unsigned verses, confirms they are not in the Knave's handwriting, and reads them to the court.
The cardboard Knave denies writing the unsigned verses, while the King and Queen treat them as evidence against him before the dream trial ends without a verdict.

The King examines Alice, tries unsuccessfully to expel her with an implausible rule, and twists meaningless verses into evidence before repeatedly demanding a verdict; the dream trial ends unresolved.
The Queen declares the Knave guilty without proof, strikes Bill with an inkstand, demands sentencing before a verdict, and renews Alice's execution order before Alice awakens.
